glossary.definition: A part of computer science focused on developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes like reasoning and learning from experience.
glossary.sentence: Artificial intelligence deals with developing hardware and software solutions endowed with human-similar intellectual processes, such as the ability to reason, discover meaning, generalise, or learn from experience (Copeland, 2020).

glossary.definition: A form of intelligence not based on a brain and nervous system where an organism gathers and updates diverse information about its environment, integrates this with information on its present internal state, and then makes decisions that reconcile its well-being with its environment.
glossary.sentence: “A plant continually gathers and updates diverse information about its environment, integrates this with information on its present internal state, and then makes decisions that reconcile its well-being with its environment” (Trewavas, 2016)

glossary.definition: An ideal intelligence adopted as a standard for definitions of artificial intelligence.
glossary.sentence: the categories on the right encompass definitions that adopt an ideal intelligence called rationality as a standard (Fig.4).

glossary.definition: An approach underlying systems that think humanly, which considers human being to be an information processing system.
glossary.sentence: A cognitive modelling approach underlies systems that think humanly. It considers human being to be an information processing system.

glossary.definition: A basis for systems that think rationally where knowledge is represented as logical rules to conclude or infer new knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Systems that think rationally are based on logics . As a result, knowledge is represented as logical rules, for example, if we know that “All students have a student ID” and “Alex is a student”, than we can conclude (to infer new knowledge) that “Alex has a student ID”.

glossary.definition: A fundamental test in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence by seeing if a tester can differentiate which answers were given by a human being and which ones by the artificial intelligence.
glossary.sentence: The Turing test underlies systems that act humanly and it is also one of the fundamental tests in the field of artificial intelligence intended to assess if a computer system possesses intelligence.

glossary.definition: An entity, such as a robot, self-driving car, or game-playing software, that operates in a changing environment and learns to act based on feedback to maximise an end goal.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is suitable for agents (like robots, self-driving cars, or a game- playing software) who operate in a changing environment and, to act in the environment, they need to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal (Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: An agent that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.
glossary.sentence: A rational agent is one that acts to achieve the best outcome or, when there is uncertainty, the best expected outcome.

glossary.definition: A chapter of artificial intelligence concerned with how language is related to thinking.
glossary.sentence: Studies of how language is related to thinking contributed to natural language processing and knowledge representation in the field of artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: The theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems, defined as the incorporation of formally described knowledge into an intelligent system to support its intelligent performance.
glossary.sentence: In general, “ knowledge representation is the theory and practice of representing knowledge for computer systems” (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A capability of a computer system to use stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions.
glossary.sentence: automated reasoning, in order to use the stored information for answering questions and drawing conclusions;

glossary.definition: The field concerned with developing a mathematical model of data.
glossary.sentence: Summarising the content of this topic, it is worth emphasising again that machine learning is about developing a mathematical model of data.

glossary.definition: A test that brings forward two more requirements for a computer: computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: However, the so called the Total Turing test brings forward two more requirements (Russell and Norvig, 2009) : the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: A capability that allows a computer to perceive objects.
glossary.sentence: the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: A capability that allows a computer to manipulate objects and move about.
glossary.sentence: the computer must have computer vision to perceive objects, and robotics to manipulate objects and move about.

glossary.definition: A scientific field that contributed to artificial intelligence by considering the idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge encoded in an internal language.
glossary.sentence: Philosophy - Philosophers made possible artificial intelligence by considering an idea that the human mind is similar to a physical device that operates knowledge.

glossary.definition: A formal science that provided tools for logical statements, uncertain probabilistic judgments, and reasoning algorithms in the areas of computing, logics, and probability theory.
glossary.sentence: Mathematics - The transition from the philosophy to the formal science required mathematical formalization in three main areas: computing, logics, and probability theory.

glossary.definition: A field that offered theories on how to make choices leading to desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents.
glossary.sentence: Economics - It offered theories on how to make choices that lead to the desired results and laid the grounds for studies of rational agents.

glossary.definition: A field related to research on how the human brain processes information, which led to the emergence of artificial neural networks.
glossary.sentence: Neuroscience – Contributions of neuroscience in artificial intelligence is related to research on how the human brain processes information.

glossary.definition: Biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain and used for supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks.
glossary.sentence: According to (Jones, 2009), “neural networks are biologically motivated computing structures that are conceptually modeled after the brain.”

glossary.definition: A field that reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines.
glossary.sentence: Psychology - Psychologists reinforced the idea that humans and animals may be seen as information-processing machines.

glossary.definition: A field that provided the speed and memory capacity necessary for the development of complex and large artificial intelligence applications.
glossary.sentence: Computer engineering – Computer engineering provided artefacts, which made possible development of artificial intelligence applications.

glossary.definition: A field that contributed the feedback theory to artificial intelligence, suggesting that intelligent behavior results from feedback mechanisms.
glossary.sentence: Control science and cybernetics - The main contribution of this field to artificial intelligence is related to the feedback theory, which offered an idea that all intelligent behaviour is the result of feedback mechanism, that is, a mechanism which can be simulated also by the computer.

glossary.definition: The idea that all intelligent behaviour is the result of a feedback mechanism that can be simulated by a computer.
glossary.sentence: The main contribution of this field to artificial intelligence is related to the feedback theory, which offered an idea that all intelligent behaviour is the result of feedback mechanism, that is, a mechanism which can be simulated also by the computer.

glossary.definition: A field that showed language use fits the model of data processing machines and studied how language relates to thinking.
glossary.sentence: Linguistics - Linguists showed that the use of language fits the model of psychologists that humans and animals can be considered as data processing machines.

glossary.definition: A goal of artificial intelligence related to understanding the principles that make intelligent performance possible in both natural and artificial systems.
glossary.sentence: scientific goal is related to understanding of those principles which make possible intelligent performance both in natural and artificial systems.

glossary.definition: A goal of artificial intelligence related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts.
glossary.sentence: engineering goal is related to the development of useful intelligent artefacts.

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems that considers the use of knowledge in reasoning and learning to be crucial in generating intellectual behaviour.
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence considers the use of knowledge in reasoning and learning to be crucial in generating intellectual behaviour, whereas connectionist artificial intelligence attributes this to the discovery of relationships in data (with little or no prior knowledge) (Goel, 2021) .

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain where knowledge is considered a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units and encoded as numerical values in the weights of the connections.
glossary.sentence: In connectionist artificial intelligence, there is an opposite view of the concept of knowledge: knowledge is not directly represented in the system because it is considered as a distributed quantity in a connected group of smaller processing units, rather than a quantity encoded in physical symbols (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: A hypothesis that underlies almost all practical and theoretical research in expert systems, search, planning systems and natural language processing.
glossary.sentence: Despite criticisms, the physical symbol system hypothesis underlies almost all practical and theoretical research in expert systems, search, planning systems and natural language processing (Luger, 2009) .

glossary.definition: A system capable of carrying out intelligent behaviour only when it is a system of physical symbols, including symbols, symbolic structures, and operations.
glossary.sentence: A physical symbol system includes (Bermúdez, 2014; Luger, 2009; Poole and Mackworth, 2010) : symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way.

glossary.definition: Meaningful physical patterns that can represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts, and causality.
glossary.sentence: symbols that correspond to meaningful physical patterns and could represent any form of knowledge, experience, concepts and causality

glossary.definition: Collections of symbols connected to each other that depict important aspects of a problem domain.
glossary.sentence: symbolic structures, which consist of symbols that are connected to each other in some way.

glossary.definition: Actions such as constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying that allow the use and creation of symbolic structures.
glossary.sentence: operations (constructing, copying, modifying, and destroying) which allow the use of existing symbolic structures and the creation of new structures

glossary.definition: An argument by John Searle describing a situation where a physical system correctly manipulates symbols according to formal rules without having actual intelligence or understanding.
glossary.sentence: John Searle’s Chinese room argument is a famous example of criticism.

glossary.definition: Also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, it is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is concerned with developing solutions for specific tasks such as chess playing or medical diagnosis.
glossary.sentence: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) .

glossary.definition: Also known as strong artificial intelligence or human-level intelligence, it treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated and is capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains.
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) .

glossary.definition: An intelligence that surpasses human capabilities and is smarter than the best human minds in fields including scientific creativity, general wisdom, and social skills.
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .

glossary.definition: An approach based on the hypothesis that an intelligent system's representations must be grounded in the physical world, focusing on organisms and processes that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales.
glossary.sentence: Brooks (1990) , the founder of this approach, has emphasised that the new artificial intelligence is based on the hypothesis that in order to build an intelligent system, it is necessary that their representations are grounded in the physical world, since the world is the best model of itself.

glossary.definition: A paradigm under new artificial intelligence that considers collective intelligence as a behaviour emerging through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents.

glossary.definition: A sub-direction of swarm intelligence that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by the food-searching behaviour of ants.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: A sub-direction of swarm intelligence that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by the behavior of birds and fish.
glossary.sentence: It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence methods that do not use knowledge about a specific problem but rely on general methods like search, logic, and automatic reasoning.
glossary.sentence: In the first module of the study course, we considered two groups of artificial intelligence methods: weak methods, which do not use knowledge about a problem under consideration, but use general methods like search, logic, syntax-based computation, automatic reasoning.

glossary.definition: Artificial intelligence methods based on a great amount of knowledge about the specific problem under consideration.
glossary.sentence: Incorporating knowledge into an intelligent computer system belongs to the so-called strong methods of artificial intelligence.

glossary.definition: Systems developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or to cope with different types of problems.
glossary.sentence: At the beginning of the study course, you learnt out that intelligent computer systems are being developed to support humans in the completion of various functions and activities, or, in other words, to cope with different types of problems.

glossary.definition: A way of structural organization of data about the real world.
glossary.sentence: When developing any computer system, including intelligent computer systems, we need to think about: data structures or, in other words, a way of structural organization of data about the real world;

glossary.definition: Places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others.
glossary.sentence: data repositories or places where to store the data, for example, files, databases, and others;

glossary.definition: Ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.
glossary.sentence: algorithms or ways to process data in order to achieve a specific goal.

glossary.definition: A mathematical representation or formal specification that uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.
glossary.sentence: The formal representation uses mathematical concepts to represent objects of the real world and their behaviour in such a way that they can be easily transformed into computational objects.

glossary.definition: The process of determining a state we are in at present (initial state), a state we would like to reach (goal state), and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4).

glossary.term: initial state of the problem-solving process
glossary.definition: The state a person or system is in at present.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4).

glossary.term: goal state of the problem-solving process
glossary.definition: The state a person or system would like to reach.
glossary.sentence: The definition of the problem means that we determine a state we are in at present (the so-called initial state of the problem-solving process ), a state we would like to reach (the so-called goal state of the problem-solving process ) and whether there is a discrepancy between these two states (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: A set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.
glossary.sentence: A state is a set of values of properties for a given object or phenomenon at the specified time.

glossary.definition: A graph representing game states, which can be complete or partial.
glossary.sentence: Although we have applied the minimax algorithm and alpha-beta pruning to a complete state space graph in this game, these principles can also be used in strategies for processing a partial state space graph.

glossary.definition: Elements of a state space graph connected by arcs whose quality is measured by heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: In the state space graph, we have only nodes and the arcs connecting them.

glossary.definition: A node in the path that has no ancestor.
glossary.sentence: However, there is no ancestor for the root node, and the algorithm therefore goes down again, via the first unconsidered path.

glossary.definition: A node that corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process and does not have descendants.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph should have at least one terminal (leaf) node , which corresponds to a goal state of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that do not have descendants;

glossary.definition: Nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process and have both ancestors and descendants.
glossary.sentence: a state space graph may have internal nodes that correspond to intermediate states of a problem-solving process. From the structural viewpoint, these are nodes that have both ancestors and descendants.

glossary.definition: The connections between nodes in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: In the state space graph, we have only nodes and the arcs connecting them.

glossary.definition: The set of nodes in a state space graph corresponding to states in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: N is the set of nodes. These correspond to states in a problem-solving process;

glossary.definition: The set of arcs between nodes in a state space graph corresponding to steps or actions in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: A is the set of arcs between nodes. These correspond to steps/actions in a problem-solving process;

glossary.definition: A nonempty subset of N containing root nodes or initial state(s) of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: IS is a nonempty subset of N. It contains root nodes or initial state(s) of a problem-solving process;

glossary.definition: A nonempty subset of N containing leaf nodes or goal state(s) of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: GS is a nonempty subset of N. It contains leaf nodes or goal state(s) of a problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: Represented by arcs in a state space graph, actions are undertakings determined by humans to solve problems and define a course of problem-solving together with intermediate states.
glossary.sentence: In the state space graph actions are represented by arcs, and together with intermediate states they define a course of problem-solving.

glossary.definition: A process occurring in the real world that includes states and actions, which can be formally represented to allow intelligent computer systems to solve problems.
glossary.sentence: So, a problem-solving process, which is a part of our real world, includes states and actions.

glossary.definition: A system that uses formal representations like state space graphs to solve problems or play games against humans or other systems.
glossary.sentence: This topic will explain to you how we can use a state space graph to represent games and thus to create an intelligent computer system that is able to play games against a human or against another intelligent computer system.

glossary.term: Zero-sum deterministic two-person games with perfect information
glossary.definition: A type of game where two players compete and make alternate moves, both have access to complete information, no randomness is involved, and one player's loss equals the other's gain.
glossary.sentence: The following aspects characterise this type of game: two players participate in the game and compete against each other... one player’s loss is equal to another player’s gain, giving a total sum of zero.

glossary.term: Deterministic two-player games of perfect information
glossary.definition: Games such as checkers or chess where both players have all information about the course of the game and random factors are not involved.
glossary.sentence: Deterministic two-player games of perfect information, such as checkers, tic-tac-toe and chess, in which both players have all the information about the course of the game... and random factors are not involved.

glossary.definition: Games involving randomness, such as the use of a die, where players cannot make precise predictions about the next move.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic two-player games of perfect information, such as backgammon, in which both players still are aware of what is happening on the playing board but a die is used, meaning that the players cannot make a precise prediction about the score on the die in the next move.

glossary.definition: A condition in games where part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information, such as bridge and poker, in which part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players (i. e. the players can see only their own cards)...

glossary.definition: The state at the beginning of a problem-solving process, such as a blank grid at the start of a game.
glossary.sentence: First, we determine the state that we are in at present (the initial state of the problem-solving process).

glossary.definition: The specific state a player or system aims to reach to complete a problem-solving process or win a game.
glossary.sentence: Then, we decide on the state we would like to reach (the goal state of the problem-solving process).

glossary.definition: Actions performed by players that change the appearance of the game state and lead to transitions between states.
glossary.sentence: To reach a goal state, the players make moves by putting their marks in empty squares.

glossary.definition: Properties related to a computer implementation of a game whose values change after a move and allow players to track the course of the game at any specific moment.
glossary.sentence: As a result, before creating a state space graph, the developer of an intelligent computer system should determine the parameters that characterise the states in the game.

glossary.definition: An aspect of the graph that must be reduced, for example by avoiding multiple representations of the same state, to prevent overconsumption of computational resources.
glossary.sentence: The complexity of a state space graph is a critical aspect. It is vital to reduce the complexity to avoid overconsumption of computational resources.

glossary.definition: A process that sequentially explores paths in a state space graph to find a solution path by selecting, checking, and expanding nodes.
glossary.sentence: The search principles explained previously have shown that while searching for a solution path, the search algorithm sequentially explores different paths in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A known node in the state space graph from which the search begins.
glossary.sentence: The search start node and the search goal node are known for the search purpose.

glossary.definition: The target node that the algorithm seeks to find within the search space.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node.

glossary.definition: The sequence of nodes and arcs from the start node to the goal node returned as the output of a search algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The algorithms provide a solution path as their output.

glossary.definition: Nodes that the search algorithm has already generated, but their descendants or ancestors have not yet been generated.
glossary.sentence: As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: Nodes whose descendants or ancestors have already been generated.
glossary.sentence: closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: A technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred, starting with a problem description or data added to working memory.
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A search process where an expert system starts with a goal or hypothetical solution and the inference engine attempts to find evidence to prove it.
glossary.sentence: In backward chaining , an expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: Nodes representing goal states in a complete state space graph where it is possible to identify a game result.
glossary.sentence: In the case of a complete state space graph, a search-based game algorithm can be applied to the whole graph; in this case, root nodes and leaf nodes are clearly represented, and it is possible to identify a game result at each of the leaf nodes.

glossary.definition: A search approach suggested when a goal is given or easily formulated, when problem data must be acquired by the algorithm, or when many rules match problem data and produce increasing conclusions.
glossary.sentence: Goal-driven search is suggested if (Luger, 2009) : A goal is given in the problem statement or it can easily be formulated.

glossary.definition: The orientation of the search process, such as choosing between descendants or ancestors, determined by data availability, ease of goal definition, and graph structure.
glossary.sentence: To select a search direction, it is necessary to analyse a problem by taking the following factors into account: availability of data about the problem; easiness of defining a search goal; the structure of the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A loop formed by connected nodes that can cause a search algorithm to get stuck and fail to find the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: In this case, the search algorithm can get stuck in the cycle and never find the search goal node.

glossary.definition: A meta-strategy and algorithm that systematically tries paths in a state space graph by returning to the most recent node with unexamined descendants or ancestors if a goal is not reached.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking is nowadays considered to be a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, because its ideas are used by other search algorithms that are able to find a solution path in a more effective way than Backtracking.

glossary.definition: A state in which a player wins the game, such as the maximiser winning with a higher score or the minimiser winning with the highest score.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm reaches a leaf node. It assigns a precise heuristic measure of 1 to the leaf node, since this is a state in which the maximiser wins the game with a higher score.

glossary.definition: Search algorithms that receive a start node, a goal node, and a state space graph to find a solution path through selecting, checking, and expanding nodes.
glossary.sentence: In Module 2 of our course, we considered the operational steps of uninformed search algorithms. They receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data, the state space graph, a data structure, is used to search for a problem solution, and they provide a solution path as their output.

glossary.definition: A synonym for uninformed search algorithms, characterized by sequential exploration of the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.

glossary.definition: Another term for uninformed search algorithms which process only nodes and arcs without knowing if one node is better than another.
glossary.sentence: This kind of search is also called brute force search or blind search, because these algorithms explore the state space graph sequentially.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that use heuristic measures and an evaluation function to select the best nodes or order them during the search process.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms receive an additional input - the principles of calculation of the heuristic measures of nodes or, in other words, a heuristic evaluation function.

glossary.definition: A measure in heuristic search, often represented as numerical values, that considers if a specific node leads to the search goal node and how quickly it does so.
glossary.sentence: The quality is measured by considering if a specific node leads to the search goal node and how quickly it leads to the search goal node.

glossary.definition: The node located on the first position in the OPEN list that the algorithm takes to process and check if it is the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list—the node A—and it becomes the current node.

glossary.definition: A high-level approach, such as Backtracking, whose ideas are used by other search algorithms to find solution paths more effectively.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking is nowadays considered to be a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, because its ideas are used by other search algorithms that are able to find a solution path in a more effective way than Backtracking.

glossary.definition: A search method that starts with the first unconsidered path on the left and moves deep into the graph until reaching a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm searches the state space graph using a depth-first search (Ertel, 2011), in which it starts with the first unconsidered path on the left and goes deep inside the state space graph until it reaches a leaf node or a node with a precise heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that explores the state space graph through a principle of deepening but stops if the search goal node is located below a specified depth limit.
glossary.sentence: At the same time, it is not able to find a solution path if the search goal node is located below the depth limit.

glossary.definition: A search method that operates in a level-by-level fashion where the shortest path is found sooner because it consists of a smaller number of arcs, though it has a serious shortcoming related to the consumption of computing resources.
glossary.sentence: The reason is that when searching in a level-by-level fashion, the shortest path will be found sooner because it consists of a smaller number of arcs, which means that the search goal node is located at higher levels.

glossary.definition: A modification of Breadth-First Search that reduces the consumption of computational resources by performing a search simultaneously in both data-driven and goal-driven directions until it finds a point of contact.
glossary.sentence: Bidirectional Search is a modification of Breadth-First Search.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that always selects the best node based on heuristic measures, choosing a node whose measure is better than the current node's measure.
glossary.sentence: It should be noted that Hill Climbing always selects the best node.

glossary.definition: A search algorithm used as a benchmark for speed in reaching the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: Although this algorithm may lead to the search goal node faster than Best-First Search, there are not any guidelines on how the beam width should be chosen.

glossary.definition: A compromise algorithm between Hill Climbing and Best-First Search that selects a limited number of best nodes for expansion in each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Beam Search is a compromise between Hill Climbing, which expands only one node per iteration, and Best-First Search, which stores and incrementally expands all nodes found before.

glossary.definition: The process where a search algorithm generates paths by considering the descendants or ancestors of a node.
glossary.sentence: It will determine that this path does not lead to the search goal node, and the search algorithm will continue expanding nodes and will generate the path A->B->G->K.

glossary.definition: Nodes generated by expanding a current node during a search iteration.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm expands the current node A by generating its three descendants—nodes D, H, and E.

glossary.definition: Nodes that precede a current node in the state space graph, which may be examined depending on the search direction.
glossary.sentence: it reaches a leaf node that is not the search goal node, then it backtracks to the most recent node on the current path having unexamined descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction) and continues searching;

glossary.definition: Algorithms that follow three main operational steps: selecting the current node, checking if the current node is the search goal node, and expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: They have three main operational steps: selecting the current node; checking if the current node is the search goal node; expanding the current node if it is not the search goal node.

glossary.definition: A data structure that stores a set of elements in sequential order.
glossary.sentence: A list stores a set of elements in sequential order.

glossary.definition: A list where the search start node and other generated nodes are added together with their heuristic measures before being selected for expansion.
glossary.sentence: The search start node is added to the OPEN list together with its heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: A list containing nodes that have already been expanded or generated nodes that were not selected as the best nodes according to the beam width.
glossary.sentence: The node A is moved to the CLOSED list as an already expanded node.

glossary.definition: A type of data structure used in the implementation of Depth-First Search where new nodes are added to the left side of the OPEN list, following the Last in, First Out principle.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the left side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack.

glossary.definition: A principle where the last data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: A stack works according to the “Last in, First Out” principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 8).

glossary.definition: A problem where a state space graph has many node levels, causing Depth-First Search to search for a long time in depth before returning to higher levels.
glossary.sentence: Besides that, the algorithm has another serious shortcoming—the problem of so-called indefinitely deep paths . It means that in a situation where the state space graph has many node levels, Depth-First Search will search for the search goal node for a long time in depth before it returns to higher levels.

glossary.definition: A threshold used in Depth-Limited Search to prevent the algorithm from following indefinitely deep paths.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search overcomes a problem of going along indefinitely deep paths.

glossary.definition: Labels added to nodes in both lists that allow the algorithm to determine if it can generate descendants/ancestors or if the node should be considered a leaf/root node.
glossary.sentence: Depth labels allow the algorithm "to understand" if it is possible to generate descendants/ancestors of the current node or if the current node should be considered a leaf/root node.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to nodes where the search start node is 0 and subsequent nodes are calculated by adding 1 to the depth level of the current node.
glossary.sentence: The depth level of the search start node is equal to 0. The depth level of any other node is calculated by adding 1 to the depth level of the current node.

glossary.definition: A value calculated by adding 1 to the depth of the current node.
glossary.sentence: Its depth is calculated by adding 1 to the depth of the current node (the node D) and is equal to 2.

glossary.definition: A type of data structure used for the OPEN list in Breadth-First Search where new nodes are added to the right side and processed according to the "First in, First Out" principle.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the right side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a queue.

glossary.definition: A principle where the first data element entered into a structure is the first one to be processed.
glossary.sentence: A queue works according to the “First in, First Out” principle: the first data element entered is the first to be processed (Figure 18).

glossary.definition: Experience-based rules that allow people to solve problems, make decisions, and reason in a more effective way, often described as a rule of thumb that leads to acceptable outcomes with less effort.
glossary.sentence: Heuristics (or heuristic knowledge) are experience-based rules that allow people to solve problems, make decisions, and reason in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: A widely used concept that corresponds to what is understood by heuristic knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Today, the widely used concept of “life hack” corresponds to what is understood by heuristic knowledge.

glossary.definition: A function applied to terminal nodes to acquire heuristic measures that are then propagated back up to the current state node.
glossary.sentence: It applies a heuristic evaluation function and acquires heuristic measures for the terminal nodes.

glossary.definition: Problem-specific knowledge, often based on experience, used to solve problems and dependent on the nature of the problem.
glossary.sentence: To solve problems, we use problem-specific knowledge called heuristic knowledge that very often is based on our experience.

glossary.definition: Measures acquired for terminal nodes that indicate the quality of a game state and are propagated up the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Following this, the heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on the principles of the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: Measured by the possibility of reaching the search goal node from a given node and the possibility of reaching it along the best (usually the shortest) path.
glossary.sentence: The quality of nodes is measured by two factors: the possibility of reaching the search goal node from the given node n or, in other words, checking if the given node n is located on graph paths leading to the search goal node, and the possibility of reaching the search goal node from the given node n by going along the best (usually the shortest) path.

glossary.definition: An abstract representation of a heuristic evaluation function calculated as the sum of g(n) and h(n).
glossary.sentence: This idea is represented in an abstract way as follows (Luger, 2009): f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where n – a node under consideration;

glossary.definition: The distance between the search start node and node n, used by the algorithm to choose shorter available paths.
glossary.sentence: g(n) – the distance between the search start node and node n; this value allows the algorithm to choose the shorter paths that are available;

glossary.definition: A heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node.
glossary.sentence: h(n) – a heuristic measure that characterizes the quality of node n or, in other words, the estimation of the distance between the node n and the search goal node;

glossary.definition: Elements characterizing states in the problem-solving process and their importance for reaching the goal state.
glossary.sentence: However, in practical applications, the previously mentioned elements of the heuristic evaluation function are represented indirectly by focusing on factors characterizing states in the problem-solving process and their importance for reaching the goal state (Figure 3).

glossary.definition: A representation of the heuristic evaluation function where weights are applied to heuristic measures of factors characterizing states.
glossary.sentence: As a result, the heuristic evaluation function can be represented as a polynomial function, as used by Samuel in his checker program ((Samuel, 1959) as cited in (Luger, 2009))

glossary.definition: The weight of the ith factor in a polynomial heuristic evaluation function.
glossary.sentence: where n – a node under consideration; ai – the weight of the ith factor; xi – the heuristic measure of the ith factor characterizing states in the problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: The heuristic measure of the ith factor characterizing states in the problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: where n – a node under consideration; ai – the weight of the ith factor; xi – the heuristic measure of the ith factor characterizing states in the problem-solving process.

glossary.definition: A game where an intelligent computer system can apply a heuristic evaluation function based on the number of potentially winning positions for the computer.
glossary.sentence: Imagine we are building an intelligent computer system that will play a game of Tic-tac-toe.

glossary.definition: A game with 8 numbered tiles and 9 squares where players move a tile onto an empty square to achieve a goal state.
glossary.sentence: Another example is the game of 8-puzzle.

glossary.term: Number of tiles out of place
glossary.definition: A factor used to evaluate states in the 8-puzzle game by comparing the number of tiles not in their goal position.
glossary.sentence: The simplest factor to consider is the number of the tiles out of place in each state when it is compared with the goal state (Figure 6).

glossary.definition: A factor used to evaluate nodes in the 8-puzzle game by summing the distances of tiles from their intended places.
glossary.sentence: Another factor that can be used to evaluate nodes is to sum up distances of tiles from their intended places (Figure 7).

glossary.definition: A factor in the 8-puzzle game meaning the change of places of two tiles located close to each other.
glossary.sentence: We can define one more factor for this game: the number of pairs of tile reversals (Figure 8).

glossary.term: Total cost of the problem’s solution
glossary.definition: The sum of the cost of calculating heuristic measures and the number of nodes visited during a search.
glossary.sentence: The total cost of the problem’s solution is the cost of the calculation of heuristic measures together with the number of visited nodes.

glossary.definition: A state space graph where only a part is generated to a specified depth because limited computational resources make it impossible to store and process the whole graph.
glossary.sentence: Only a part of a state space graph (a partly available state space graph) . It is not possible to store and process the whole graph due to limited computational resources.

glossary.definition: A problem where limited look-ahead depth fails to detect a heuristically promising path, potentially leading to a bad situation later in a game.
glossary.sentence: One of the most significant problems with this approach is called the horizon effect (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009).

glossary.definition: A state space graph where computational resources are sufficient to store and process the entire graph structure.
glossary.sentence: A full state space graph (a fully available state space graph) . Computational resources are enough to store and process such a graph.

glossary.definition: The average number of arcs per node in a state space graph, calculated by dividing the sum of direct descendants of all nodes by the total number of nodes.
glossary.sentence: B – the branching factor of the state space graph . It is the average number of arcs per node.

glossary.definition: The length of the longest path in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: L – depth of the state space graph (usually the length of the longest path).

glossary.definition: A characteristic used to transform a state space graph by reducing the number of states based on identical properties, such as in Tic-tac-toe.
glossary.sentence: One of the suggestions is to use a symmetry feature of states, which can be done for the game of Tic-tac-toe (Figure 12).

glossary.definition: A heuristic search algorithm that always moves to the best node in the state space graph without implementing backtracking.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to nodes by an intelligent computer system or evaluation function to allow the selection of promising moves or to identify game results.
glossary.sentence: In both cases, the intelligent computer system assigns heuristic measures to the nodes of the available graph, and these allow the intelligent computer system to select promising moves to make during game play.

glossary.definition: A shortcoming where an algorithm fails to reach the goal because it finds a node with a better heuristic measure than its descendants or ancestors.
glossary.sentence: the local maximum problem : the algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction );

glossary.definition: A shortcoming where an algorithm cannot continue the search because the heuristic measure of the best descendant or ancestor is equal to the measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: A data structure used for the OPEN list where the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.
glossary.sentence: The priority queue works according to the principle that the node with the best heuristic measure is processed first.

glossary.definition: A defined value used by an algorithm to select the best nodes from a set of evaluated nodes based on their heuristic measures.
glossary.sentence: According to the defined beam width, the algorithm selects the two best nodes based on the heuristic measures.

glossary.term: Deterministic two-player zero-sum game of perfect information
glossary.definition: A type of game whose implementation selection depends on whether the state space graph is completely or partially available.
glossary.sentence: The selection of an algorithm for the implementation of a deterministic two-player zero-sum game of perfect information depends on the availability of the state space graph, i.e. whether it is completely or partially available.

glossary.definition: A graph that includes all states of a game, from the initial state represented by the root node to all goal states represented by leaf nodes.
glossary.sentence: A complete state space graph includes all states, from the initial state of the game (represented by the root node of the graph), to all goal states of the game (represented by leaf nodes).

glossary.definition: A complex case where game results cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.
glossary.sentence: A partial state space graph is a more complex case, since the results of the game cannot be evaluated at the terminal nodes of the sub-graph.

glossary.definition: Nodes located at a predefined depth in a game's state space that are evaluated using a heuristic function.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm goes down from the current state of the game to the terminal nodes located at the predefined depth.

glossary.definition: A participant whose task is to win the game.
glossary.sentence: the task of the maximiser is to win the game.

glossary.definition: A participant whose task is to minimise the game result for the maximiser.
glossary.sentence: the task of the minimiser is to minimise the game result for the maximiser.

glossary.definition: A level in the state space graph where a node acquires the maximum heuristic measure from its direct descendants.
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MAX level, and thus acquires the maximum heuristic measure from its direct descendants.

glossary.definition: A level in the state space graph where a node's heuristic measure is determined based on its descendants, typically where the minimiser acts.
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MIN level, but there is no difference between the heuristic measures of the descendants.

glossary.definition: The maximum heuristic measure acquired by a node at the MAX level from its direct descendants.
glossary.sentence: The current node is located at the MAX level, and thus acquires the maximum heuristic measure from its direct descendants. This is a game result, and we can see that it is the same as provided by the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: A path starting at the root node and reaching a leaf node where all nodes are closely related and have heuristic measures equal to the game result.
glossary.sentence: A winning path is a path that starts at the root node and reaches a leaf node while satisfying the following conditions: all nodes in the winning path are closely related; all nodes in the winning path have the same heuristic measures, which are equal to the game result.

glossary.definition: A set of principles or rules used to propagate heuristic measures from terminal nodes back up to the current state node.
glossary.sentence: Following this, the heuristic measures are propagated back up to the node of the current state, based on the principles of the minimax algorithm.

glossary.definition: Rules used to propagate acquired heuristic measures up to the current node in a state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Acquired heuristic measures are propagated up to the current node using the rules of the minimax algorithm or alpha-beta pruning.

glossary.definition: A search method that may be impractical if the sub-graph is too large to be analyzed within a specific time limit.
glossary.sentence: In this situation, it is not possible to use N-ply look-ahead, since a sub-graph may be so huge that the time limit could expire before it can be analysed.

glossary.definition: A strategy used under time limits that explores a partial state space graph by going one level deeper at each iteration.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner – at each iteration, it goes one level deeper (Rich et al., 2009).

glossary.definition: Paths identified using game results, which are the same as those for the minimax algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Using the game results, winning paths can be identified (Figure 9).

glossary.definition: A value assigned to nodes at MAX levels that defines the best move to maximise the result of the game and should not decrease.
glossary.sentence: An alpha number (α) is assigned to nodes at MAX levels.

glossary.definition: A value assigned to nodes at MIN levels that defines the best move to minimise the result of the game and should not increase.
glossary.sentence: A beta number (β) is assigned to nodes at MIN levels.

glossary.definition: A procedure performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node when the value of β is smaller than α.
glossary.sentence: By comparing the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of β is smaller than α, meaning that an α-cut-off should be performed by cutting off unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A procedure performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node when the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor.
glossary.sentence: the α value of the current node is equal to the β value of the ancestor, meaning that a β-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: A measure assigned to a node based on its level and the known heuristic measures of its direct descendants.
glossary.sentence: it therefore assigns a precise heuristic measure based on the level of the current node and the known heuristic measures of the direct descendants.

glossary.definition: A measure assigned when a precise measure cannot be determined, indicating potential best and worst-case outcomes for a player.
glossary.sentence: It is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure in this case, and the algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1, since the node is located at the MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: An intermediate heuristic measure of a node or ancestor used in comparison with a β value to determine if a cut-off should be performed.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the α value) is 1 and the measure of the ancestor (the β value) is 1.

glossary.definition: An intermediate heuristic measure of a node or ancestor used in comparison with an α value to determine if a cut-off should be performed.
glossary.sentence: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the β value) is −1, and the measure of the ancestor (the α value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The study of computers and computing, including theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware, software, and their uses for processing information.
glossary.sentence: It is “the study of computers and computing, including their theoretical and algorithmic foundations, hardware and software, and their uses for processing information” (Tucker & Belford, 2019).

glossary.definition: One of the three specific types of algorithms included within the field of machine learning.
glossary.sentence: It includes three types of algorithms – supervised, unsupervised and reinforcement learning algorithms.

glossary.definition: One of the three specific types of algorithms included within the field of machine learning.
glossary.sentence: It includes three types of algorithms – supervised, unsupervised and reinforcement learning algorithms.

glossary.definition: One of the three specific types of algorithms included within the field of machine learning.
glossary.sentence: It includes three types of algorithms – supervised, unsupervised and reinforcement learning algorithms.

glossary.definition: Algorithms that are explicitly programmed by developers as a set of exact and complete instructions to fulfill a task based on known inputs.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a conventional algorithm is a set of exact and complete instructions to fulfil a task.

glossary.definition: A particular unit of data, also known as a data point or observation, typically represented by a number of feature values.
glossary.sentence: A data object (also called a data point, an observation, an example) - a particular unit of data typically represented by a number of feature values...

glossary.definition: A collection of data objects.
glossary.sentence: A dataset - a collection of data objects, for example, a set of data about patients who attended Hospital A between January and May in 2019.

glossary.definition: A measurable characteristic or property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A feature (also called an attribute) - a measurable characteristic/property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm...

glossary.definition: A specific value assigned to a feature for a particular data object.
glossary.sentence: A feature value – a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is “the patient’s temperature”, then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.

glossary.definition: A set of all features that characterize a single data object.
glossary.sentence: A feature set – a set of all features characterising a data object...

glossary.definition: An external configuration variable set by the developer before training, such as the learning rate.
glossary.sentence: A hyperparameter (also called a model hyperparameter) - an external configuration variable whose value is typically set by the developer arbitrarily before the training...

glossary.definition: A mathematical representation of the mapping between input and output data in a machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A model - a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: The result produced after applying a mathematical model to specific data objects.
glossary.sentence: A model output - a result of applying a model to given data objects.

glossary.definition: An internal configuration variable of a model whose value is learned by the algorithm during the training process.
glossary.sentence: A paramete r (also called a model parameter) - an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.

glossary.definition: A set of data used in the testing process to check the performance of a model.
glossary.sentence: The model training based on the given training set leads to the acquisition of the trained model that is further used in the model testing based on the test set.

glossary.definition: The process of evaluating a model's performance using data it has not previously seen.
glossary.sentence: Testing – the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: The process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.
glossary.sentence: Training - the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.

glossary.definition: A subset of the dataset used in the training of the model, which in supervised learning already contains labels representing the desired solution or answer.
glossary.sentence: A training set - a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.

glossary.definition: The first step in producing a machine learning model, involving the study of a specific use case and corresponding business needs to reveal reasons for building the model and the tasks it will support.
glossary.sentence: Step 1 is about framing the problem by studying a specific use case and corresponding business needs.

glossary.definition: A process that typically includes replacing missing or invalid data, data normalisation, identifying key features, reducing dimensionality, and transforming textual data into numerical data.
glossary.sentence: Data pre-processing (Step 4) typically includes replacing missing or invalid data, data normalisation, identifying key features, reducing dimensionality, transforming textual data into numerical data, etc.

glossary.definition: A set of rules or processes that can simplify code, perform better than traditional problem-solving approaches, adapt to new data in fluctuating environments, and find underlying patterns and structure in large amounts of data.
glossary.sentence: One machine learning algorithm can often simplify code and perform better.

glossary.definition: Parameters of artificial neural networks such as the number of layers, the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, and the activation function to be used.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neural networks have many hyperparameters: the number of layers, the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, and the activation function to be used.

glossary.definition: The result acquired from model training based on a given training set, which is further used in model testing.
glossary.sentence: The model training based on the given training set leads to the acquisition of the trained model that is further used in the model testing based on the test set.

glossary.definition: A type of learning where a mathematical model is created based on input data that already contain labels representing the desired solution or answer.
glossary.sentence: The first topic of this module explained that, in supervised learning, the mathematical model is created based on input data that already contain labels representing the desired solution or answer.

glossary.definition: Data components representing the desired solution or answer for a specific data object.
glossary.sentence: In supervised learning, input data (the training set) already contain labels representing the desired solution or answer (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: Models that predict the output for a new data object based on the model trained on a training set.
glossary.sentence: Thus, supervised learning is based on predictive models because it predicts the output for a new data object based on the model trained on the training set.

glossary.definition: Deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs.
glossary.sentence: classification that means deciding the category to which an input object represented by its feature values belongs;

glossary.definition: Searching for a cause-and-effect relationship between two variables: an independent one and a dependent one.
glossary.sentence: In general, the concept of regression means searching for a cause-and-effect relationship between two variables: independent one and dependent one (Figure 1).

glossary.definition: A type of machine learning that uses unlabelled datasets to reveal the structure of data.
glossary.sentence: In the first topic of Module 4, you learnt that unsupervised learning uses unlabelled datasets and reveals the structure of data.

glossary.definition: Models provided by unsupervised learning that reveal patterns or structure of data and can be used for data visualisation.
glossary.sentence: Unsupervised learning provides descriptive models.

glossary.definition: The process of dividing a dataset into subsets that share some similarities.
glossary.sentence: One of the tasks of unsupervised machine learning is clustering .

glossary.definition: Subsets of data characterized by the similarity of objects within them and their difference from objects in other subsets.
glossary.sentence: These subsets of data are called clusters ; for example, an enterprise can group its clients based on the level of income (clients with high, average, and low income), a university can group students based on the average mark (students with marks in the range of 10-8, 7-6, and 5-4), a librarian can group books based on a theme (romantic books, science fiction, etc.).

glossary.definition: An unsupervised learning task that finds rules based on common co-occurring values of data objects.
glossary.sentence: Association analysis finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019)

glossary.definition: An unsupervised learning task associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, typically by dropping less important features or merging correlated features.
glossary.sentence: Dimensionality reduction is associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019)

glossary.definition: A reward-based type of learning where an agent learns how to act in a changing environment to find the best sequence of actions that maximise the end goal.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning is reward-based learning.

glossary.definition: A numerical value given as feedback to an agent when it acts correctly.
glossary.sentence: The agent receives feedback: a reward if it acts correctly and a penalty if it acts incorrectly (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: Feedback given to an agent when it acts incorrectly.
glossary.sentence: The agent receives feedback: a reward if it acts correctly and a penalty if it acts incorrectly (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: The data processing that leads to a specific output, such as a class label in classification or a prediction of a specified property in regression.
glossary.sentence: a decision process: the data processing that leads to a specific output: in a classification task – a class label, in a regression task - a prediction of the value of a specified property of a new data object;

glossary.definition: A component that measures how good the output is by comparing it to known examples.
glossary.sentence: an error function: measuring how good the output is by comparing it to known examples;

glossary.definition: A process that reduces classification or prediction error by updating variables of the mathematical model used by the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: an updating or optimisation process: reducing the classification or prediction error by updating variables of the mathematical model used by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: A supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression that assumes a linear relationship between input variables and a single output variable.
glossary.sentence: It is a supervised learning algorithm performing the task of regression. It assumes the linear relationship between the input variables (values of data object features) and the single output variable (a prediction).

glossary.definition: Feature values of data objects used in a machine learning model.
glossary.sentence: Input variables are feature values of data objects.

glossary.definition: A prediction made by a model, which in linear regression is a real number ranging from minus infinity to infinity.
glossary.sentence: The output variable is a prediction made by the linear regression model.

glossary.definition: A real number corresponding to the intercept, which is the point where the line crosses the y axis, or the value of y when x is equal to 0.
glossary.sentence: It is also usually called a bias term/coefficient .

glossary.definition: A set of real-valued values, one for each input, that express the strength or importance of each neuron input's influence on the output and serve as the basic means of long-term memory.
glossary.sentence: Weights are the basic means of long-term memory in artificial neural networks.

glossary.definition: The difference between the right output and the predicted output.
glossary.sentence: Thus, there is a difference between the right output and the predicted output. It is called an error.

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression where only one single feature is used to represent a data object.
glossary.sentence: univariate (or simple) linear regression – when only one single feature is used to represent a data object and the simple equation of the linear regression model is used

glossary.definition: A type of linear regression where each data object is represented by an n-element vector of features.
glossary.sentence: multivariate (multiple) linear regression – when each data object is represented by an n-element vector of features and the more complex equation of the linear regression model

glossary.definition: A loss function used for a single data object in linear regression.
glossary.sentence: The linear regression model uses a loss function called squared loss (also known as L2 loss) (Google Developers, n.d.; Russell and Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: A performance evaluation metric that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.

glossary.definition: A model where there is a unique solution because the weights can be calculated using specific equations.
glossary.sentence: For univariate linear regression, there is a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using the following equations (Russell and Norvig, 2010):

glossary.definition: An optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values, based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables.
glossary.sentence: The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values.

glossary.definition: A hyperparameter representing the rate at which linear regression updates the weights of the linear function, associated with how fast the algorithm converges to the error minima.
glossary.sentence: To apply the gradient descent, a hyperparameter α called learning rate is used. It represents a rate at which Linear regression updates the weights of the linear function.

glossary.definition: A type of classification where there are only two possible classes/categories, represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive).
glossary.sentence: Binary classification is when there are only two possible classes/categories which are represented by labels 0 (negative) and 1 (positive );

glossary.definition: A classification task where recall is calculated for each specific class by taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the row as false negatives.
glossary.sentence: In the multiclass classification, we first calculate a recall for each specific class, taking the value on the diagonal as true positives and the sum of other values in the row corresponding to the class as false negatives.

glossary.definition: A supervised machine learning algorithm used for classification tasks, especially binary classification, which predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression , in its turn, is used for the classification task, especially a binary classification task. Therefore, it predicts one of two classes/categories to which a data object belongs to...

glossary.definition: A function applied to squash the value of the linear function within the range of [0,1].
glossary.sentence: Still, after receiving the linear function's value, it applies a sigmoid function to squash the value of teh linear function within the range of [0,1] using the sigmoid function.

glossary.definition: A value of 0.5 at which the sigmoid function crosses the y axis, used to decide about a class/category of a data object.
glossary.sentence: The threshold value of 0.5 at which the function crosses the y axis is used to decide about a class/category of a data object.

glossary.definition: A simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks by finding the most similar neighbour in the existing dataset and assigning its category/class label to the new data object.
glossary.sentence: K-nearest neighbour or kNN algorithm is a very simple supervised learning algorithm that can solve both classification and regression tasks.

glossary.definition: A metric, such as the distance between data objects, used to determine the similarity of data objects based on feature-based descriptions.
glossary.sentence: To determine the similarity of data objects, a similarity metric is used such as the distance between data objects because similar data objects are close to each other.

glossary.definition: A metric used for the calculation of distances between data objects, specifically applied to continuous feature values.
glossary.sentence: Figure 12 shows the examples of calculation of the Euclidean and Manhattan distances for two data objects.

glossary.definition: A metric used for the calculation of distances between data objects, specifically applied to continuous feature values.
glossary.sentence: Figure 12 shows the examples of calculation of the Euclidean and Manhattan distances for two data objects.

glossary.definition: Data features represented as discrete categories or classes.
glossary.sentence: For categorical feature values, which are represented as discrete categories/classes, the Hamming distance is used that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: A distance metric that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ; it is used for categorical feature values.
glossary.sentence: For categorical feature values, which are represented as discrete categories/classes, the Hamming distance is used that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: Datasets that include both continuous and categorical feature values.
glossary.sentence: As quite usually datasets include mixed-type data – both continuous and categorical, to cope with these data, we can rely on the sum of the squared distances along corresponding features (Kubat, 2017): Figure 14 demonstrates the calculation of the distance for mixed-type feature values.

glossary.definition: A version of the nearest neighbour algorithm that classifies a new data object by finding a single, closest data object in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closet data object in the dataset (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: A version of the algorithm that uses a hyperparameter k to define how many nearest data objects are considered to identify the output value for a new data object.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used.

glossary.definition: A value set by the developer before the algorithm operates that defines the number of nearest data objects considered to identify an output value.
glossary.sentence: It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.

glossary.definition: A process where the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and takes the plurality vote of the neighbours to determine a label.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification) (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: The most frequent class among k labels, returned by the algorithm in classification tasks.
glossary.sentence: identify the output for the new data object: in the classification task, the algorithm will return the mode of the k labels (the most frequent class); in the regression task, it returns the mean of the k labels.
